content.body: Advanced differentiation extends your S4 skills to more complex functions. Three essential rules allow you to differentiate any combination of functions.

Chain Rule:  d/dx[f(g(x))] = f'(g(x)) · g'(x)
Product Rule: d/dx[u·v] = u'v + uv'
Quotient Rule: d/dx[u/v] = (u'v - uv') / v²

content.title: Chain Rule Example
content.body:
Differentiate y = (3x² + 1)⁵
Let u = 3x² + 1, so y = u⁵
dy/du = 5u⁴,  du/dx = 6x
dy/dx = 5(3x² + 1)⁴ · 6x = 30x(3x² + 1)⁴

content.title: Product Rule Example
content.body:
Differentiate y = x³ sin(x)
u = x³, v = sin(x)
u' = 3x², v' = cos(x)
dy/dx = 3x² sin(x) + x³ cos(x)
Always identify u and v clearly before applying the product or quotient rule. This prevents sign errors.

questions.question: Using the chain rule, what is d/dx[(2x+3)⁴]?
What is d/dx[x² · eˣ]?
questions.explanation: Product rule: u'v + uv' = 2x·eˣ + x²·eˣ = eˣ(2x+x²)

questions.question: The quotient rule states d/dx[u/v] equals:
Differentiate y = sin(x²). What is dy/dx?

content.body: Integration by substitution is the reverse of the chain rule. Integration by parts handles products of functions.

Substitution: ∫f(g(x))·g'(x)dx = ∫f(u)du where u = g(x)

Integration by Parts: ∫u dv = uv - ∫v du
Choose u using LIATE: Logarithm, Inverse trig, Algebraic, Trig, Exponential

content.title: Substitution Example
content.body:
Find ∫2x(x²+1)⁴ dx
Let u = x²+1,  du = 2x dx
∫u⁴ du = u⁵/5 + C = (x²+1)⁵/5 + C

content.title: Integration by Parts
content.body:
Find ∫x·eˣ dx
u = x,  dv = eˣ dx
du = dx,  v = eˣ
∫x·eˣ dx = x·eˣ - ∫eˣ dx = x·eˣ - eˣ + C = eˣ(x-1) + C

questions.question: To integrate ∫6x(3x²+1)² dx, the best substitution is:
questions.explanation: Let u = 3x²+1, then du = 6x dx, giving ∫u² du

questions.question: In integration by parts, LIATE helps you choose u. What does 'A' stand for?
questions.explanation: LIATE = Logarithm, Inverse trig, Algebraic, Trig, Exponential

questions.question: For ∫x·ln(x) dx, which choice of u is correct (LIATE)?
questions.explanation: Logarithm comes before Algebraic in LIATE, so u = ln(x)

questions.question: ∫eˣ dx equals:
questions.explanation: The integral of eˣ is itself: eˣ + C
